text: Yet you say that there is such a thing as pleasure and pain.
pali: ‘Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, natthi sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘na kho, timbaruka, natthi sukhadukkhaṁ;

text: ‘When name and form don’t exist, there is no consciousness.
I say that choices have a vital condition, they don’t lack a vital condition.
pali: Saṅkhārepāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanise vadāmi, no anupanise.

text: Craving is the vital condition for grasping.
That’s why an unlearned ordinary person is unable to become disillusioned, dispassionate, or freed from it.
pali: Tasmā tatrāssutavā puthujjano nālaṁ nibbindituṁ nālaṁ virajjituṁ nālaṁ vimuccituṁ.

text: ‘The source of this attachment is craving. When craving exists attachments come to be. And when craving doesn’t exist attachments don’t come to be.’
pali: ‘upadhi taṇhānidāno taṇhāsamudayo taṇhājātiko taṇhāpabhavo, taṇhāya sati upadhi hoti, taṇhāya asati upadhi na hotī’ti.

text: “Ignorance is a requirement for choices.
“What are old age and death, sir, and who do they belong to?”
pali: “‘katamaṁ nu kho, bhante, jarāmaraṇaṁ, kassa ca panidaṁ jarāmaraṇan’ti?

text: Then it occurred to me:
“Well, knowing and seeing thus do you, with clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far?”
pali: “Api pana tumhe āyasmanto evaṁ jānantā evaṁ passantā dibbāya sotadhātuyā visuddhāya atikkantamānusikāya ubho sadde suṇātha dibbe ca mānuse ca ye dūre santike cā”ti?

text: “And when there’s no grasping at all, would continued existence still be found?”
pali: “Sabbaso vā pana upādāne asati, upādānanirodhā api nu kho bhavo paññāyethā”ti.

text: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī.
and the practice that leads to their cessation.
The sense fields of the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind.
pali: Cakkhāyatanaṁ, sotāyatanaṁ, ghānāyatanaṁ, jivhāyatanaṁ, kāyāyatanaṁ, manāyatanaṁ—

text: ‘The Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples is practicing the way that’s good, direct, systematic, and proper. It consists of the four pairs, the eight individual persons. This is the Saṅgha of the Buddha’s disciples that is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of greeting with cupped palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.’
pali: ‘suppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, ujuppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, ñāyappaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, sāmīcippaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, yadidaṁ cattāri purisayugāni aṭṭha purisapuggalā, esa bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho āhuneyyo pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā’ti.

text: When feeling exists craving comes to be.
Then Vipassī thought:
pali: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vipassissa bodhisattassa etadahosi:

text: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
“In water, sir.”
Craving is a requirement for grasping. …
I say that suffering has a vital condition.
pali: Dukkhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.

text: Knowledge of name and form …
“Yes, reverend,” replied Sāriputta. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him,
pali: “Evaṁ, āvuso”ti kho āyasmā sāriputto tassa bhikkhuno paṭissutvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ bhagavā etadavoca:

text: He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.”
pali: “Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā”ti abbhaññāsi.

text: Nose consciousness arises dependent on the nose and smells. …
Hence it would be cogent to ask:
pali: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:

text: when rebirth ceases, old age and death cease.”
Their craving will grow.
‘Continued existence is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of rebirth.’
pali: ‘jāti kho, āvuso, bhavanidānā bhavasamudayā bhavajātikā bhavappabhavā’ti.

text: “Reverend Musīla, apart from faith, endorsement, oral transmission, reasoned train of thought, or acceptance of a view after deliberation, do you personally know that
pali: “Aññatreva, āvuso musila, saddhāya aññatra ruciyā aññatra anussavā aññatra ākāraparivitakkā aññatra diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā atthāyasmato musilassa paccattameva ñāṇaṁ:

text: grasping …
Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way:
pali: Ete te, timbaruka, ubho ante anupagamma majjhena tathāgato dhammaṁ deseti:

text: when the six sense fields cease, contact ceases …
Sir, let the Blessed One explain suffering to me!
pali: Ācikkhatu ca me, bhante, bhagavā dukkhaṁ.

text: “But Sāriputta, suppose they were to ask you:
“It’s not that there’s no such thing as pleasure and pain.
When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease.
At Sāvatthī.
Where there is no rebirth, old age, and death in the future, I say there’s no sorrow, anguish, and distress.
pali: Yattha natthi āyatiṁ jātijarāmaraṇaṁ, asokaṁ taṁ, bhikkhave, adaraṁ anupāyāsanti vadāmi.

text: ‘Suffering includes whatever is felt.’
If you have the view that the soul is one thing, the body another, there is no living of the spiritual life.
pali: ‘Aññaṁ jīvaṁ aññaṁ sarīraṁ’ iti vā, bhikkhave, diṭṭhiyā sati brahmacariyavāso na hoti.

text: When you rub two sticks together, heat is generated and fire is produced. But when you part the sticks and lay them aside, any corresponding heat ceases and stops.
pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dvinnaṁ kaṭṭhānaṁ saṅghaṭṭanasamodhānā usmā jāyati tejo abhinibbattati. Tesaṁyeva dvinnaṁ kaṭṭhānaṁ nānākatavinibbhogā yā tajjā usmā sā nirujjhati sā vūpasammati;

text: For a third time …
pali: Tatiyampi kho bhagavā āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ āmantesi:

text: In the same way, ignorance is the vital condition for choices. … Freedom is the vital condition for the knowledge of ending.”
pali: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, avijjūpanisā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārūpanisaṁ viññāṇaṁ, viññāṇūpanisaṁ nāmarūpaṁ, nāmarūpūpanisaṁ saḷāyatanaṁ, saḷāyatanūpaniso phasso, phassūpanisā vedanā, vedanūpanisā taṇhā, taṇhūpanisaṁ upādānaṁ, upādānūpaniso bhavo, bhavūpanisā jāti, jātūpanisaṁ dukkhaṁ, dukkhūpanisā saddhā, saddhūpanisaṁ pāmojjaṁ, pāmojjūpanisā pīti, pītūpanisā passaddhi, passaddhūpanisaṁ sukhaṁ, sukhūpaniso samādhi, samādhūpanisaṁ yathābhūtañāṇadassanaṁ, yathābhūtañāṇadassanūpanisā nibbidā, nibbidūpaniso virāgo, virāgūpanisā vimutti, vimuttūpanisaṁ khaye ñāṇan”ti.

text: “Well, Reverend Sāriputta, is continued existence made by oneself? …” …
pali: “Kiṁ nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, sayaṅkato bhavo …pe…

text: In the same way, name and form are requirements for consciousness.
So I have heard.
for three,
pali: tīṇi rattindivāni cepi maṁ bhagavā etamatthaṁ puccheyya …pe…

pali: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme …

pali: idaṁ nu kho dukkhaṁ kiṁnidānaṁ kiṁsamudayaṁ kiṁjātikaṁ kiṁpabhavaṁ?

text: “And what is dependent origination?
then he explains, teaches, asserts, establishes, clarifies, analyzes, and reveals it.
pali: Abhisambujjhitvā abhisametvā ācikkhati deseti paññāpeti paṭṭhapeti vivarati vibhajati uttānīkaroti.

text: Consciousness …
When craving ceases, grasping ceases. …
“Worthy Gotama, does all exist?”
“No, sir.”
‘But what is the source of this grasping? …’ …
Rebirth is a requirement for old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress to come to be.
“Ask what you wish, Kassapa.”

‘Will I exist in the future? Will I not exist in the future? What will I be in the future? How will I be in the future? After being what, what will I become in the future?’
pali: ‘bhavissāmi nu kho ahaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, nanu kho bhavissāmi anāgatamaddhānaṁ, kiṁ nu kho bhavissāmi anāgatamaddhānaṁ, kathaṁ nu kho bhavissāmi anāgatamaddhānaṁ, kiṁ hutvā kiṁ bhavissāmi nu kho ahaṁ anāgatamaddhānan’ti;

text: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
When name and form don’t exist the six sense fields don’t come to be. …
Feeling is a requirement for craving.
‘In this way our going forth will not be barren, but will be fruitful and fertile.
pali: ‘Evaṁ no ayaṁ amhākaṁ pabbajjā avañjhā bhavissati saphalā saudrayā.

text: These are called the forty-four grounds for knowledge.
The six sense fields are requirements for contact.
Craving is a requirement for grasping. …

Saying this you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
pali: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva me assa, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.

text: their cessation,
Suppose there was a great tree.
This is called death.
“Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
pali: “Tena hi, bhikkhave, suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.

text: Fed and fuelled by that, the great tree would stand for a long time.
pali: Evañhi so, bhikkhave, mahārukkho tadāhāro tadupādāno ciraṁ dīghamaddhānaṁ tiṭṭheyya.

text: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of attachments.
pali: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno upadhinirodhāya.

text: ‘Did I exist in the past? Did I not exist in the past? What was I in the past? How was I in the past? After being what, what did I become in the past?’
pali: ‘ahosiṁ nu kho ahaṁ atītamaddhānaṁ, nanu kho ahosiṁ atītamaddhānaṁ, kiṁ nu kho ahosiṁ atītamaddhānaṁ, kathaṁ nu kho ahosiṁ atītamaddhānaṁ, kiṁ hutvā kiṁ ahosiṁ nu kho ahaṁ atītamaddhānan’ti;

text: If they’re freed by not grasping, by disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding ignorance, they’re qualified to be called ‘a mendicant who has attained extinguishment in this very life’.”
pali: Avijjāya ce bhikkhu nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādāvimutto hoti, ‘diṭṭhadhammanibbānappatto bhikkhū’ti alaṁvacanāyā”ti.

text: consciousness …
That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
Choices are a requirement for consciousness …
These three feelings are impermanent,
pali: Imā kho, āvuso, tisso vedanā aniccā.

text: This is called old age.
The Buddha said this:
In the same way, pleasant feeling arises dependent on a contact to be experienced as pleasant.
pali: evameva kho, bhikkhave, sukhavedaniyaṁ phassaṁ paṭicca uppajjati sukhavedanā.

text: Why is that?
When he said this, Sāriputta kept silent.
pali: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā sāriputto tuṇhī ahosi.

text: ‘When what doesn’t exist, what is not? Due to the cessation of what, what ceases?
pali: ‘kiṁ nu kho kismiṁ asati kiṁ na hoti, kissa nirodhā kiṁ nirujjhati?

text: and you do see suffering.
In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.”
pali: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjatī”ti.

text: ‘When the six sense fields exist there’s contact. The six sense fields are requirements for contact.’
pali: ‘saḷāyatane kho sati phasso hoti, saḷāyatanapaccayā phasso’ti.

text: Then it occurred to me:
Then, through rational application of mind, Vipassī penetrated with wisdom:
pali: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vipassissa bodhisattassa yoniso manasikārā ahu paññāya abhisamayo:

text: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha. “You might say, ‘What are old age and death, and who do they belong to?’ Or you might say, ‘Old age and death are one thing, who they belong to is another.’ But both of these mean the same thing, only the phrasing differs.
pali: ‘No kallo pañho’ti bhagavā avoca, ‘katamaṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, kassa ca panidaṁ jarāmaraṇan’ti iti vā, bhikkhu, yo vadeyya, ‘aññaṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ aññassa ca panidaṁ jarāmaraṇan’ti, iti vā, bhikkhu, yo vadeyya, ubhayametaṁ ekatthaṁ byañjanameva nānaṁ.

text: In this way the sapling is cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, obliterated, and unable to arise in the future.
pali: Evañhi so, bhikkhave, taruṇo rukkho ucchinnamūlo assa tālāvatthukato anabhāvaṅkato āyatiṁ anuppādadhammo.

text: ‘What are name and form …’
“No, sir.”
And as their suffering grows, they are not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are not freed from suffering, I say.
pali: Ye dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhenti te na parimuccanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘na parimuccanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.

pali: Tasseva adukkhamasukhavedaniyassa phassassa nirodhā yaṁ tajjaṁ vedayitaṁ adukkhamasukhavedaniyaṁ phassaṁ paṭicca uppannā adukkhamasukhavedanā sā nirujjhati sā vūpasammati.

text: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. …
pali: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, tadapi phassapaccayā.

text: “Yes, sir,” they replied.
pali: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.

Seeing this, one is freed by not grasping through disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding the fuel for its origination.
pali: Tadāhārasambhavanti yathābhūtaṁ sammappaññāya disvā āhārasambhavassa nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimutto hoti.

“It’s incredible, Reverend Sāriputta, it’s amazing!

text: When ignorance fades away and ceases with no residue left behind, choices cease.

“It’s not that I don’t know or see suffering.
pali: ‘Na khvāhaṁ, kassapa, dukkhaṁ na jānāmi, na passāmi.

text: rebirth is a requirement for old age and death.”
In the same way, that which is called ‘mind’ and also ‘sentience’ and also ‘consciousness’ arises as one thing and ceases as another all day and all night.
pali: evameva kho, bhikkhave, yamidaṁ vuccati cittaṁ itipi, mano itipi, viññāṇaṁ itipi, taṁ rattiyā ca divasassa ca aññadeva uppajjati aññaṁ nirujjhati.

text: The king’s men did as they were told.
‘When consciousness doesn’t exist there is no name and form.
Dependent on contact.
Suffering is real.”
Where consciousness is established and grows, name and form are conceived.
pali: Yattha patiṭṭhitaṁ viññāṇaṁ virūḷhaṁ, atthi tattha nāmarūpassa avakkanti.

text: “Venerable sir,” they replied.
They don’t completely understand choices,
I do know pleasure and pain,
the cessation of continued existence is extinguishment?”

text: “Sir, if they were to ask me this,

A noble disciple comes to truly understand the origin and ending of the world. Such a noble disciple is one who is called ‘one accomplished in view’, ‘one accomplished in vision’, ‘one who has come to the true teaching’, ‘one who sees this true teaching’, ‘one endowed with a trainee’s knowledge’, ‘one who has entered the stream of the teaching’, ‘a noble one with penetrative wisdom’, and also ‘one who stands pressing against the door to freedom from death’.”
pali: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ lokassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako diṭṭhisampanno itipi, dassanasampanno itipi, āgato imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, passati imaṁ saddhammaṁ itipi, sekkhena ñāṇena samannāgato itipi, sekkhāya vijjāya samannāgato itipi, dhammasotaṁ samāpanno itipi, ariyo nibbedhikapañño itipi, amatadvāraṁ āhacca tiṭṭhati itipī”ti.

text: You should say: ‘Faith.’
“No, Reverend Koṭṭhita, old age and death are not made by oneself, nor by another, nor by both oneself and another, nor do they arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
pali: “Na kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, sayaṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, na paraṅkataṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ, na sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca jarāmaraṇaṁ, nāpi asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ jarāmaraṇaṁ.

text: Knowledge of rebirth …
pali: jātiyā ñāṇaṁ, jātisamudaye ñāṇaṁ, jātinirodhe ñāṇaṁ, jātinirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya ñāṇaṁ;

text: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
(Saviṭṭha repeats exactly the same series of questions, and Nārada answers just as Musīla did.)
pali: Aññatreva, āvuso nārada, saddhāya aññatra ruciyā aññatra anussavā aññatra ākāraparivitakkā aññatra diṭṭhinijjhānakkhantiyā atthāyasmato nāradassa paccattameva ñāṇaṁ:

text: They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation.

text: “Is perception permanent or impermanent?”
“Mendicants, form of the past and future is not-self,
When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed.”
I’ve analytically taught the four kinds of mindfulness meditation,
‘This bag of bones is my last.’
This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.” …
pali: Imassa kho, bhikkhu, mayā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa evaṁ vitthārena attho daṭṭhabbo”ti …pe…

text: It’s not ‘was’ or ‘will be’.
pali: na tassa saṅkhā ‘ahosī’ti, na tassa saṅkhā ‘bhavissatī’ti.

text: “Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.”
pali: “sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā saṅkhittena dhammaṁ desetu yamahaṁ bhagavato dhammaṁ sutvā eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto vihareyyan”ti.

text: At Sāvatthī.
“What do you think, Ānanda?
Still, they have such a view:
That’s how it is, Tissa,
perception …
So when that feeling of theirs decays and perishes,
pali: Tassa ‘ahaṁ vedanā, mama vedanā’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, sā vedanā vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.

text: “Mendicants, I went in search of form’s gratification,

Any kind of consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or fine; inferior or superior; far or near, which is accompanied by defilements and fuels grasping: this is called the grasping aggregate of consciousness.
pali: Yaṁ kiñci viññāṇaṁ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṁ …pe… yaṁ dūre santike vā sāsavaṁ upādāniyaṁ, ayaṁ vuccati viññāṇupādānakkhandho.

text: “Reverend, an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t truly understand the origin, the disappearance, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape when it comes to form,
pali: “Idhāvuso, assutavā puthujjano rūpassa samudayañca atthaṅgamañca assādañca ādīnavañca nissaraṇañca yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti.

text: or choices
And where do these three unskillful thoughts cease without remainder?
pali: Ime ca bhikkhave, tayo akusalavitakkā kva aparisesā nirujjhanti?

text: but its cessation, settling, and ending is not brittle.
pali: Yo tassa nirodho vūpasamo atthaṅgamo, idaṁ appabhaṅgu.

text: This is called perception.
When that person went to the householder or householder’s son and offered to
pali: yadā hi so puriso amuṁ gahapatiṁ vā gahapatiputtaṁ vā upasaṅkamitvā evaṁ āha:

text: consciousness as self.
They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’”
There are these six classes of intention:
They neither get rid of nor accumulate form, but remain after getting rid of it.
pali: Rūpaṁ nevācināti na apacināti, apacinitvā ṭhito;

text: “I can, Ānanda,” said the Buddha.

text: And how does a mendicant grounded on ethics develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path?

text: And who is practicing wrongly?

pali: Appamattassetaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno pāṭikaṅkhaṁ—ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvessati ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarissati.

text: What five?
“Mendicants, these eight things don’t arise to be purified, bright, flawless, and rid of corruptions apart from the Holy One’s training.
pali: “Aṭṭhime, bhikkhave, dhammā parisuddhā pariyodātā anaṅgaṇā vigatūpakkilesā anuppannā uppajjanti, nāññatra sugatavinayā.

text: If wanderers of other religions were to ask you:

text: It’s good friendship.

pali: evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno ariyassa aṭṭhaṅgikassa maggassa uppādāya etaṁ pubbaṅgamaṁ etaṁ pubbanimittaṁ, yadidaṁ—sīlasampadā.

text: should cleanse themselves of mental corruptions.

pali: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kalyāṇamitto ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarotī”ti.

text: “These are all terms for the noble eightfold path:
pali: “imasseva kho etaṁ, ānanda, ariyassa aṭṭhaṅgikassa maggassa adhivacanaṁ:

text: “… accomplishment in view …”
“Mendicants, suppose a pot full of water is tipped over, so the water drains out and doesn’t go back in.
pali: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, kumbho nikkujjo vamateva udakaṁ, no paccāvamati;

text: Sounds known by the ear …
‘Reverends, what’s the goal of leading the spiritual life under the ascetic Gotama?’
This is called the goal of the spiritual path.”
“Mendicants, there are these three searches.
“Mendicants, I do not see a single thing that gives rise to the noble eightfold path, or, if it’s already arisen, fully develops it like good friendship.
pali: “Nāhaṁ, bhikkhave, aññaṁ ekadhammampi samanupassāmi, yena anuppanno vā ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo uppajjati, uppanno vā ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchati, yathayidaṁ, bhikkhave, kalyāṇamittatā.

text: That’s how a mendicant who is diligent develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.”
pali: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu appamatto ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarotī”ti.

text: That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities.”
pali: evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlaṁ nissāya sīle patiṭṭhāya ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvento ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaronto mahantattaṁ vepullattaṁ pāpuṇāti dhammesū”ti.

text: They are: right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.

text: It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sammādiṭṭhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ …pe… sammāsamādhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ …

text: This is called the spiritual path.

pali: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu appamatto ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaroti?

text: They are right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
This is called life as a brahmin.
In the same way accomplishment in ethics is the forerunner and harbinger of the noble eightfold path for a mendicant. …”
pali: evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno ariyassa aṭṭhaṅgikassa maggassa uppādāya etaṁ pubbaṅgamaṁ etaṁ pubbanimittaṁ, yadidaṁ—sīlasampadā …pe…

text: That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.”
pali: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvento ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaronto nibbānaninno hoti nibbānapoṇo nibbānapabbhāro”ti.

text: Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
pali: Cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā,

text: “Mendicants, I will teach you life as a brahmin and the fruits of life as a brahmin.

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sammādiṭṭhiṁ bhāveti …pe… sammāsamādhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ …

text: And how does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment?
pali: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvento ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaronto nibbānaninno hoti nibbānapoṇo nibbānapabbhāro?

text: What five?
It is right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
“… whose objective, destination, and culmination is freedom from death …”
The ending of greed, hate, and delusion.
pali: Yo kho, bhikkhave, rāgakkhayo dosakkhayo mohakkhayo—

text: It’s someone who has right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco sammādiṭṭhiko hoti, sammāsaṅkappo, sammāvāco, sammākammanto, sammāājīvo, sammāvāyāmo, sammāsati, sammāsamādhi—

text: So I have heard.

“Indeed, in answering this way you repeat what I’ve said, and don’t misrepresent me with an untruth. Your explanation is in line with the teaching, and there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal or criticism.
pali: “Taggha tumhe, bhikkhave, evaṁ puṭṭhā evaṁ byākaramānā vuttavādino ceva me hotha, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkhatha, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākarotha, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānuvādo gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgacchati.

text: There’s feeling conditioned by thought.
It is right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
“Mendicants, these eight things don’t arise to be purified, bright, flawless, and rid of corruptions except when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has appeared.
pali: “Aṭṭhime, bhikkhave, dhammā parisuddhā pariyodātā anaṅgaṇā vigatūpakkilesā anuppannā uppajjanti, nāññatra tathāgatassa pātubhāvā arahato sammāsambuddhassa.

text: And what’s the right practice?
And those whose minds are rightly developed
This is called the goal of the ascetic life.”
And how does a mendicant who develops the noble eightfold path slant, slope, and incline to extinguishment?
pali: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvento ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaronto nibbānaninno hoti nibbānapoṇo nibbānapabbhāro?

text: They are: right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
right speech …
pali: Sammāvācā, ānanda, bhāvitā bahulīkatā rāgavinayapariyosānā hoti, dosa …pe… mohavinayapariyosānā hoti.

text: “Mendicant, it’s someone who has a trainee’s right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
pali: “Idha, bhikkhu, sekkhāya sammādiṭṭhiyā samannāgato hoti …pe… sekkhena sammāsamādhinā samannāgato hoti.

text: “Mendicants, there are these three searches.
That’s how a mendicant who develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path slants, slopes, and inclines to extinguishment.”
pali: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvento ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaronto nibbānaninno hoti nibbānapoṇo nibbānapabbhāro”ti.

text: ‘The goal of leading the spiritual life under the Buddha is the fading away of greed.’
“Mendicants, the dawn is the forerunner and harbinger of the sunrise.
pali: “Sūriyassa, bhikkhave, udayato etaṁ pubbaṅgamaṁ etaṁ pubbanimittaṁ, yadidaṁ—aruṇuggaṁ;

text: they delight in not grasping.
The noble eightfold path should be developed to give up …”
pali: Imāsaṁ kho, bhikkhave, tissannaṁ esanānaṁ pahānāya ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo bhāvetabbo.

text: This is another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.”
pali: Iminā kho etaṁ, sāriputta, pariyāyena veditabbaṁ yathā sakalamidaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, yadidaṁ—kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā”ti.

text: What five?
What eight?
“Mendicants, there are these three searches.
“Mendicants, the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the ocean.
pali: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, gaṅgā nadī samuddaninnā samuddapoṇā samuddapabbhārā;

text: That’s how a mendicant grounded on ethics develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.”
pali: evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlaṁ nissāya sīle patiṭṭhāya ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarotīti.

text: I praise right practice for laypeople and renunciates.
pali: Gihino vāhaṁ, bhikkhave, pabbajitassa vā sammāpaṭipadaṁ vaṇṇemi.

text: “… accomplishment in diligence …”
And what is right speech?
“… accomplishment in self-development …”
A mendicant accomplished in ethics can expect to develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path. …” …
What eight?
At Sāvatthī.
It should be said: the five grasping aggregates.
This is called the ascetic life.

It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sammādiṭṭhiṁ bhāveti …pe… sammāsamādhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ …

text: This is called an even more untrue person.
This is called right speech.
And what fuels the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, āhāro anuppannassa vā vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya pāripūriyā?

text: Why is that?
For that is indeed the path that you acquired which, when developed and cultivated by you meditating in just such a way, will bring you to such a state that you will understand:
pali: Eso hi te, udāyi, maggo paṭiladdho, yo te bhāvito bahulīkato tathā tathā viharantaṁ tathattāya upanessati yathā tvaṁ:

text: (Tell in full as in SN 45.139–148, removal of greed version.)
And how are the seven awakening factors developed and cultivated so as to lead to giving up the things that tighten the fetters?
pali: Kathaṁ bhāvitā ca, bhikkhave, satta bojjhaṅgā kathaṁ bahulīkatā saṁyojanīyānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya saṁvattanti?

text: When the body is tranquil and one feels bliss, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi.
“Reverends, the ascetic Gotama teaches his disciples like this:
pali: “Samaṇo, āvuso, gotamo sāvakānaṁ evaṁ dhammaṁ deseti:

text: “Indeed, Blessed One, these are awakening factors!
(Tell in full as in SN 45.161–170.)
What seven?
‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’
pali: ‘khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā’ti pajānissāmi.

text: That’s how the perception of cessation, when developed and cultivated, leads to great benefit … great sanctuary from the yoke … great inspiration … great ease.”
pali: Evaṁ bhāvitā kho, bhikkhave, nirodhasaññā evaṁ bahulīkatā mahato atthāya saṁvattati, mahato yogakkhemāya saṁvattati, mahato saṁvegāya saṁvattati, mahato phāsuvihārāya saṁvattatī”ti.

text: and the mass of delusion for the first time. …
Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it.
pali: Tattha cakkhumā puriso sakaṁ mukhanimittaṁ paccavekkhamāno yathābhūtaṁ na jāneyya na passeyya.

text: They develop the awakening factor of immersion …
In the same way, when your heart is overcome and mired in dullness and drowsiness … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed.
pali: Evameva kho, brāhmaṇa, yasmiṁ samaye thinamiddhapariyuṭṭhitena cetasā viharati thinamiddhaparetena, uppannassa ca thinamiddhassa nissaraṇaṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti, attatthampi tasmiṁ samaye yathābhūtaṁ na jānāti na passati, paratthampi …pe… ubhayatthampi tasmiṁ samaye yathābhūtaṁ na jānāti na passati; dīgharattaṁ sajjhāyakatāpi mantā nappaṭibhanti, pageva asajjhāyakatā.

text: This body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue.
pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, ayaṁ kāyo āhāraṭṭhitiko, āhāraṁ paṭicca tiṭṭhati, anāhāro no tiṭṭhati;

text: How helpful my fondness and respect for the Buddha have been, and my sense of conscience and prudence.
pali: Yāva bahukatañca me, bhante, bhagavati pemañca gāravo ca hirī ca ottappañca.

text: Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt.
pali: Kāmacchando, bhikkhave, cittassa upakkileso, yena upakkilesena upakkiliṭṭhaṁ cittaṁ na ceva mudu hoti na ca kammaniyaṁ, na ca pabhassaraṁ pabhaṅgu ca, na ca sammā samādhiyati āsavānaṁ khayāya …pe…

text: What seven?

As a mendicant rouses up the awakening factor of mindfulness, they understand: ‘My mind is well freed. I’ve eradicated dullness and drowsiness, and eliminated restlessness and remorse. My energy is roused up, and my mind is sharply focused, not sluggish.’ …
pali: “Satisambojjhaṅgaṁ kho, āvuso, bhikkhu ārabbhamāno pajānāti ‘cittañca me suvimuttaṁ, thinamiddhañca me susamūhataṁ, uddhaccakukkuccañca me suppaṭivinītaṁ, āraddhañca me vīriyaṁ, aṭṭhiṁ katvā manasi karomi, no ca līnan’ti …pe…

text: At Sāvatthī.
Or else, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness.
pali: Sabbaso vā pana viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.

text: The treasures of the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
pali: Satisambojjhaṅgassa ratanassa pātubhāvo hoti …pe… upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa ratanassa pātubhāvo hoti.

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
When they’re strong they dive into the pools. Then they dive into the lakes, the streams, the rivers, and finally the ocean. There they acquire a great and abundant body.
pali: te tattha kāyaṁ vaḍḍhetvā balaṁ gāhetvā kusobbhe otaranti, kusobbhe otaritvā mahāsobbhe otaranti, mahāsobbhe otaritvā kunnadiyo otaranti, kunnadiyo otaritvā mahānadiyo otaranti, mahānadiyo otaritvā mahāsamuddasāgaraṁ otaranti; te tattha mahantattaṁ vepullattaṁ āpajjanti kāyena;

text: Because after hearing the teaching of such mendicants, a mendicant will live withdrawn in both body and mind,
pali: Tathārūpānaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhūnaṁ dhammaṁ sutvā dvayena vūpakāsena vūpakaṭṭho viharati—kāyavūpakāsena ca cittavūpakāsena ca.

text: Frequent rational application of mind to them
Those mendicants neither approved nor rejected that statement of the wanderers of other religions.
pali: Atha kho te bhikkhū tesaṁ aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ bhāsitaṁ neva abhinandiṁsu nappaṭikkosiṁsu.

text: These are called the things that tighten the fetters.”
There is the feature of beauty.
There is the settled mind.
This is a cause and reason for knowing and seeing.
pali: ayampi kho, rājakumāra, hetu, ayaṁ paccayo ñāṇāya dassanāya.

text: Because it’s easy to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things.
pali: Līnaṁ, bhikkhave, cittaṁ taṁ etehi dhammehi susamuṭṭhāpayaṁ hoti.

text: Not knowing and not seeing have no cause or reason.
And what are the seven awakening factors that are fully developed?
pali: Katame satta bojjhaṅgā tasmiṁ samaye bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchanti?

text: That is what the Buddha said.
They closely watch over that mind immersed in samādhi.
pali: So tathāsamāhitaṁ cittaṁ sādhukaṁ ajjhupekkhitā hoti.

text: “Mendicant, they’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.
If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do.
pali: So sace ākaṅkhati ‘appaṭikūle paṭikūlasaññī vihareyyan’ti, paṭikūlasaññī tattha viharati.

text: It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ …pe…

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
“Mendicants, the dawn is the forerunner and harbinger of the sunrise.
pali: “Sūriyassa, bhikkhave, udayato etaṁ pubbaṅgamaṁ etaṁ pubbanimittaṁ, yadidaṁ—aruṇuggaṁ;

text: What seven?
That’s how the seven awakening factors are developed and cultivated so as to fulfill knowledge and freedom.”
pali: Evaṁ bhāvitā kho, kuṇḍaliya, satta bojjhaṅgā evaṁ bahulīkatā vijjāvimuttiṁ paripūrentī”ti.

text: Then Venerable Mahācunda went up to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him:
pali: Atha kho āyasmā mahācundo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho āyasmantaṁ mahācundaṁ bhagavā etadavoca:

text: When the perception of cessation is developed and cultivated you can expect one of two results:
pali: Nirodhasaññāya, bhikkhave, bhāvitāya bahulīkatāya dvinnaṁ phalānaṁ aññataraṁ phalaṁ pāṭikaṅkhaṁ—

text: It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ …pe…

text: Not frequently applying the mind to that
What seven?
And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of energy, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, anāhāro anuppannassa vā vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya pāripūriyā?

text: If not, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re extinguished without extra effort.
pali: No ce diṭṭheva dhamme paṭikacca aññaṁ ārādheti, no ce maraṇakāle aññaṁ ārādheti, no ce pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā antarāparinibbāyī hoti, no ce pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā upahaccaparinibbāyī hoti, atha pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā asaṅkhāraparinibbāyī hoti.

text: How is the heart’s release by compassion developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and end?
pali: Kathaṁ bhāvitā panāvuso, karuṇācetovimutti, kiṅgatikā hoti, kiṁparamā, kiṁphalā, kiṁpariyosānā?

text: This is the way in which the seven awakening factors become fourteen.”
pali: Ayaṁ kho, bhikkhave, pariyāyo, yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma satta bojjhaṅgā catuddasā”ti.

text: “Mendicants, all the ascetics and brahmins in the past who have given up the three discriminations have done so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors.
pali: “Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā tisso vidhā pajahiṁsu, sabbe te sattannaṁ bojjhaṅgānaṁ bhāvitattā bahulīkatattā.

text: and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.

text: they delight in not grasping.

As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, worthy Gotama has made the teaching clear in many ways.
pali: Seyyathāpi, bho gotama, nikkujjitaṁ vā ukkujjeyya, paṭicchannaṁ vā vivareyya, mūḷhassa vā maggaṁ ācikkheyya, andhakāre vā telapajjotaṁ dhāreyya: ‘cakkhumanto rūpāni dakkhantī’ti; evameva bhotā gotamena anekapariyāyena dhammo pakāsito.

text: A mendicant with good friends can expect to develop and cultivate the seven awakening factors.
pali: Kalyāṇamittassetaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno pāṭikaṅkhaṁ—satta bojjhaṅge bhāvessati, satta bojjhaṅge bahulīkarissati.

text: When native gold is corrupted by these five corruptions it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant, but is brittle and not completely ready for working.
pali: Ime kho, bhikkhave, pañca jātarūpassa upakkilesā, yehi upakkilesehi upakkiliṭṭhaṁ jātarūpaṁ na ceva mudu hoti na ca kammaniyaṁ, na ca pabhassaraṁ pabhaṅgu ca, na ca sammā upeti kammāya.

text: “Mendicants, I will teach you seven principles that prevent decline.
So I have heard.
That’s how what is concisely referred to as ‘the hindrance of ill will’ becomes twofold.
pali: ‘Byāpādanīvaraṇan’ti iti hidaṁ uddesaṁ gacchati. Tadamināpetaṁ pariyāyena dvayaṁ hoti.

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
“It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factor of mindfulness, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go.
pali: “Idha, rājakumāra, bhikkhu satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.

text: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Haliddavasana.
pali: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā koliyesu viharati haliddavasanaṁ nāma koliyānaṁ nigamo.

text: In the same way, take a certain gentleman who has gone forth from the lay life to homelessness, abandoning sensual pleasures. But beset by sensual pleasures that are similar, or even worse, he breaks apart, collapses, and falls.
pali: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, idhekacco kulaputto yādisake kāme ohāya agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti, so tādisakehi kāmehi tato vā pāpiṭṭhatarehi obhaggavibhaggo vipatito seti.

text: (Tell in full as in SN 45.171–180.)
This is the path for crushing Māra’s army.”
Endowed with even a single awakening factor you would truly know and see, let alone all seven awakening factors.
pali: Ekamekenapi kho, bhante, bojjhaṅgena samannāgato yathābhūtaṁ jāneyya passeyya, ko pana vādo sattahi bojjhaṅgehi?

text: Leaving home behind
Frequent irrational application of mind to them
It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of cessation together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu nirodhasaññāsahagataṁ satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti …pe…

text: And what starves the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, starves its full development?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, anāhāro anuppannassa vā upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya pāripūriyā?

text: They attain enlightenment early on in this very life.
There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright.
pali: Atthi, bhikkhave, kusalākusalā dhammā sāvajjānavajjā dhammā hīnapaṇītā dhammā kaṇhasukkasappaṭibhāgā dhammā.

text: “Cunda, express your understanding of the awakening factors.”
At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of immersion; they develop it and perfect it.
pali: Yasmiṁ samaye, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno passaddhakāyassa sukhino cittaṁ samādhiyati, samādhisambojjhaṅgo tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhuno āraddho hoti; samādhisambojjhaṅgaṁ tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhu bhāveti; samādhisambojjhaṅgo tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhuno bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchati.

text: “Mendicants, when love is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …”

“Mendicants, whatever qualities are skillful, part of the skillful, on the side of the skillful, all of them are rooted in diligence and meet at diligence, and diligence is said to be the best of them.
pali: “Ye keci, bhikkhave, dhammā kusalā kusalabhāgiyā kusalapakkhikā, sabbe te appamādamūlakā appamādasamosaraṇā; appamādo tesaṁ dhammānaṁ aggamakkhāyati.

text: If they wish: ‘May I meditate staying equanimous, mindful and aware, shunning both the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do.
pali: Sace ākaṅkhati ‘appaṭikūlañca paṭikūlañca tadubhayaṁ abhinivajjetvā upekkhako vihareyyaṁ sato sampajāno’ti, upekkhako tattha viharati sato sampajāno.

text: “Mendicants, there are these five obstacles and hindrances, corruptions of the heart that weaken wisdom.
pali: “Pañcime, bhikkhave, āvaraṇā nīvaraṇā cetaso upakkilesā paññāya dubbalīkaraṇā.

text: It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ …pe…

text: “Mendicants, when you apply the mind irrationally, sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt arise, and once arisen they increase and grow.”

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.

“Mendicants, when you apply the mind rationally, the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity arise, and once they’ve arisen, they’re fully developed.”
pali: “Yoniso ca kho, bhikkhave, manasikaroto anuppanno ceva satisambojjhaṅgo uppajjati, uppanno ca satisambojjhaṅgo bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchati …pe… anuppanno ceva upekkhāsambojjhaṅgo uppajjati, uppanno ca upekkhāsambojjhaṅgo bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchatī”ti.

text: “Moggallāna, I’ve rightly explained these seven awakening factors. When developed and cultivated, they lead to direct knowledge, to awakening, and to extinguishment.
pali: “Sattime, moggallāna, bojjhaṅgā mayā sammadakkhātā bhāvitā bahulīkatā abhiññāya sambodhāya nibbānāya saṁvattanti.

text: “Mendicants, these seven awakening factors are not obstacles, hindrances, or corruptions of the mind. When developed and cultivated they lead to the realization of the fruit of knowledge and freedom.
pali: “Sattime, bhikkhave, bojjhaṅgā anāvaraṇā anīvaraṇā cetaso anupakkilesā bhāvitā bahulīkatā vijjāvimuttiphalasacchikiriyāya saṁvattanti.

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
And what starves the arising of restlessness and remorse, or, when they have arisen, starves their increase and growth?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, anāhāro anuppannassa vā uddhaccakukkuccassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā uddhaccakukkuccassa bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya?

text: Because it’s hard to settle a restless mind with these things.
pali: Uddhataṁ, bhikkhave, cittaṁ taṁ etehi dhammehi duvūpasamayaṁ hoti.

text: At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of investigation of principles;
pali: Yasmiṁ samaye, bhikkhave, bhikkhu tathā sato viharanto taṁ dhammaṁ paññāya pavicinati pavicarati parivīmaṁsamāpajjati, dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgo tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhuno āraddho hoti;

text: fuels the arising of the awakening factor of equanimity, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it.
pali: ayamāhāro anuppannassa vā upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya pāripūriyā.

text: fuels the arising of doubt, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow.
pali: ayamāhāro anuppannāya vā vicikicchāya uppādāya, uppannāya vā vicikicchāya bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya.

text: The apex of the heart’s release by love is the beautiful, I say, for a wise mendicant here who has not penetrated to a higher freedom.
pali: Subhaparamāhaṁ, bhikkhave, mettācetovimuttiṁ vadāmi, idhapaññassa bhikkhuno uttarivimuttiṁ appaṭivijjhato.

text: “In the same way, when the mind is sluggish, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
pali: “Evameva kho, bhikkhave, yasmiṁ samaye līnaṁ cittaṁ hoti, akālo tasmiṁ samaye passaddhisambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya, akālo samādhisambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya, akālo upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya.

text: What, then, is the difference between the ascetic Gotama’s teaching and instruction and ours?”
pali: Idha no, āvuso, ko viseso, ko adhippayāso, kiṁ nānākaraṇaṁ samaṇassa vā gotamassa amhākaṁ vā, yadidaṁ—dhammadesanāya vā dhammadesanaṁ, anusāsaniyā vā anusāsanin”ti?

text: They’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.”
“But what things must be developed and cultivated in order to fulfill the three kinds of good conduct?”
pali: “Katame pana, bho gotama, dhammā bhāvitā bahulīkatā tīṇi sucaritāni paripūrentī”ti?

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.
pali: Satisambojjhaṅgo kho, bhante, bhagavatā sammadakkhāto bhāvito bahulīkato abhiññāya sambodhāya nibbānāya saṁvattati …pe… upekkhāsambojjhaṅgo kho, bhante, bhagavatā sammadakkhāto bhāvito bahulīkato abhiññāya sambodhāya nibbānāya saṁvattati.

text: There is tranquility of the body and of the mind.
If they toss dry grass, cow-dung, and timber on it, blow on it, and don’t scatter dirt on it,
pali: So tattha sukkhāni ceva tiṇāni pakkhipeyya, sukkhāni gomayāni pakkhipeyya, sukkhāni kaṭṭhāni pakkhipeyya, mukhavātañca dadeyya, na ca paṁsukena okireyya;

text: What seven?

“Mendicants, sometimes a noble disciple pays attention, applies the mind, concentrates wholeheartedly, and actively listens to the teaching. At such a time the five hindrances are absent, and the seven awakening factors are fully developed.
pali: Yasmiṁ, bhikkhave, samaye ariyasāvako aṭṭhiṁ katvā manasi katvā sabbaṁ cetaso samannāharitvā ohitasoto dhammaṁ suṇāti, imassa pañca nīvaraṇā tasmiṁ samaye na honti. Satta bojjhaṅgā tasmiṁ samaye bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchanti.

text: They truly know and see with a mind that has developed the awakening factor of mindfulness.
pali: So satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāvitena cittena yathābhūtaṁ jānāti passati—

text: When they’re freed, they know they’re freed.

Meditate spreading a heart full of compassion to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, spread a heart full of compassion to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.
pali: Karuṇāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharatha, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ; iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ karuṇāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjena pharitvā viharatha.

text: Suppose someone wanted to make a small fire flare up.
pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso parittaṁ aggiṁ ujjāletukāmo assa.

text: At such a time, a mendicant has initiated the awakening factor of rapture; they develop it and perfect it.
pali: Yasmiṁ samaye, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno āraddhavīriyassa uppajjati pīti nirāmisā, pītisambojjhaṅgo tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhuno āraddho hoti; pītisambojjhaṅgaṁ tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhu bhāveti; pītisambojjhaṅgo tasmiṁ samaye bhikkhuno bhāvanāpāripūriṁ gacchati.

text: It’s when a mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
“It’s too early to wander for alms in Sāvatthī.
‘But reverends, how is the heart’s release by love developed? What is its destination, apex, fruit, and culmination?
pali: ‘kathaṁ bhāvitā panāvuso, mettācetovimutti, kiṅgatikā hoti, kiṁparamā, kiṁphalā, kiṁpariyosānā?

text: When he said this, Saṅgārava said to the Buddha,
pali: Evaṁ vutte, saṅgāravo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:

text: Even a person with clear eyes checking their own reflection wouldn’t truly know it or see it.
pali: Tattha cakkhumā puriso sakaṁ mukhanimittaṁ paccavekkhamāno yathābhūtaṁ na jāneyya na passeyya.

text: It’s when a mendicant develops the perception of cessation together with the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu nirodhasaññāsahagataṁ satisambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti …pe…

text: pay homage to them,
pali: payirupāsanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, tesaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ bahukāraṁ vadāmi;

text: “Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to great benefit.
pali: “Aṭṭhikasaññā, bhikkhave, bhāvitā bahulīkatā mahato atthāya saṁvattati.

text: These are the five corruptions of the mind. When the mind is corrupted by these it’s not pliable, workable, or radiant. It’s brittle, and not rightly immersed in samādhi for the ending of defilements.”
pali: ime kho, bhikkhave, pañca cittassa upakkilesā, yehi upakkilesehi upakkiliṭṭhaṁ cittaṁ na ceva mudu hoti na ca kammaniyaṁ, na ca pabhassaraṁ pabhaṅgu ca, na ca sammā samādhiyati āsavānaṁ khayāyā”ti.

text: At Sāvatthī.
Why is that?
What, then, is the difference between the ascetic Gotama’s teaching and instruction and ours?”
pali: Idha no, āvuso, ko viseso, ko adhippayāso, kiṁ nānākaraṇaṁ samaṇassa vā gotamassa amhākaṁ vā, yadidaṁ—dhammadesanāya vā dhammadesanaṁ, anusāsaniyā vā anusāsanin”ti?

text: “Mendicants, when the perception of repulsiveness of food is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …”
“Yes, sir.”
These seven awakening factors don’t arise to be developed and cultivated except when a Realized One, a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha has appeared.”
pali: ime kho, bhikkhave, satta bojjhaṅgā bhāvitā bahulīkatā anuppannā uppajjanti, nāññatra tathāgatassa pātubhāvā arahato sammāsambuddhassā”ti.

text: What seven?
And this is how knowing and seeing have causes and reasons.”
pali: Evaṁ sahetu, sappaccayo ñāṇaṁ dassanaṁ hotī”ti.

text: and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: upekkhāsahagataṁ upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.

text: and present who give up the three discriminations do so by developing and cultivating the seven awakening factors.”
pali: pajahanti, sabbe te imesaṁyeva sattannaṁ bojjhaṅgānaṁ bhāvitattā bahulīkatattā”ti.

text: There is discontent, sloth, yawning, sleepiness after eating, and mental sluggishness.
pali: Atthi, bhikkhave, arati tandi vijambhitā bhattasammado cetaso ca līnattaṁ.

text: That’s how sense restraint is developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the three kinds of good conduct.
pali: Evaṁ bhāvito kho, kuṇḍaliya, indriyasaṁvaro evaṁ bahulīkato tīṇi sucaritāni paripūreti.

text: (Tell in full as in SN 45.171–179.)
There are these five obstacles and hindrances, parasites of the mind that weaken wisdom.
pali: Pañcime, bhikkhave, āvaraṇā nīvaraṇā cetaso ajjhāruhā paññāya dubbalīkaraṇā.

text: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’”
pali: ‘khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā’ti pajānissasī”ti.

text: What seven?
the mass of hate,
Sensual desire,
pali: Kāmacchando, bhikkhave, āvaraṇo nīvaraṇo cetaso upakkileso paññāya dubbalīkaraṇo.

text: Suppose that a ruler or their chief minister had a chest full of garments of different colors.
pali: Seyyathāpi, āvuso, rañño vā rājamahāmattassa vā nānārattānaṁ dussānaṁ dussakaraṇḍako pūro assa.

text: And so, Udāyī, when craving ends, deeds end; when deeds end suffering ends.”
pali: Iti kho, udāyi, taṇhakkhayā kammakkhayo, kammakkhayā dukkhakkhayo”ti.

text: This is where these fetters, shackles, and attachments arise.
(Tell in full as in SN 45.139–148.)
“Mendicants, when you frequently apply the mind to things that are grounds for sensual greed, sensual desire arises, and once arisen it increases and grows.
pali: “Kāmarāgaṭṭhāniyānaṁ, bhikkhave, dhammānaṁ manasikārabahulīkārā anuppanno ceva kāmacchando uppajjati, uppanno ca kāmacchando bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya saṁvattati.

text: “Mendicants, when the perception of not-self in suffering is developed and cultivated it’s very fruitful and beneficial. …”
Such is perception …
those who practice accordingly
restlessness and remorse …
There are things that are the basis of serenity and freedom from distraction.
Or else, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that ‘consciousness is infinite’, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness.
pali: Sabbaso vā pana ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘anantaṁ viññāṇan’ti viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.

text: But sir, what is the cause and reason for knowing and seeing?
pali: Katamo pana, bhante, hetu, katamo paccayo ñāṇāya dassanāya?

text: Because it’s easy to stimulate a sluggish mind with these things.
pali: Līnaṁ, bhikkhave, cittaṁ taṁ etehi dhammehi susamuṭṭhāpayaṁ hoti.

text: What seven?
And how are the four kinds of mindfulness meditation developed and cultivated so as to fulfill the seven awakening factors?
pali: Kathaṁ bhāvitā ca, kuṇḍaliya, cattāro satipaṭṭhānā kathaṁ bahulīkatā satta bojjhaṅge paripūrenti?

text: And how does a mendicant develop the seven awakening factors depending on and grounded on ethics, acquiring great and abundant good qualities?
pali: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlaṁ nissāya sīle patiṭṭhāya satta bojjhaṅge bhāvento satta bojjhaṅge bahulīkaronto mahantattaṁ vepullattaṁ pāpuṇāti dhammesūti?

text: Then they went to the monastery of the wanderers of other religions and exchanged greetings with the wanderers there.
pali: Atha kho te bhikkhū yena aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ ārāmo tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā tehi aññatitthiyehi paribbājakehi saddhiṁ sammodiṁsu.

text: If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the unrepulsive in the repulsive and the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do.
pali: Sace ākaṅkhati ‘paṭikūle ca appaṭikūle ca appaṭikūlasaññī vihareyyan’ti, appaṭikūlasaññī tattha viharati.

text: These are the seven awakening factors.”
dullness and drowsiness,
pali: anuppannañceva thinamiddhaṁ uppajjati, uppannañca thinamiddhaṁ bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya saṁvattati;

text: The awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity.”
“It’s too early to wander for alms in Haliddavasana.
That’s how the perception of a skeleton, when developed and cultivated, is very fruitful and beneficial.”
pali: Evaṁ bhāvitā kho, bhikkhave, aṭṭhikasaññā evaṁ bahulīkatā mahapphalā hoti mahānisaṁsā”ti.

text: And the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion, and equanimity don’t arise, or if they’ve already arisen, they cease.
pali: anuppanno ceva satisambojjhaṅgo nuppajjati, uppanno ca satisambojjhaṅgo nirujjhati …pe… anuppanno ceva upekkhāsambojjhaṅgo nuppajjati, uppanno ca upekkhāsambojjhaṅgo nirujjhati.

text: “There’s a time when the heart is overcome and mired in sensual desire, without truly knowing and seeing the escape from sensual desire that has arisen.
pali: “Yasmiṁ kho, rājakumāra, samaye kāmarāgapariyuṭṭhitena cetasā viharati kāmarāgaparetena, uppannassa ca kāmarāgassa nissaraṇaṁ yathābhūtaṁ na jānāti na passati—

text: and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
pali: upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.

text: Sensual desire, ill will, dullness and drowsiness, restlessness and remorse, and doubt.
pali: Kāmacchandanīvaraṇaṁ, bhikkhave, andhakaraṇaṁ acakkhukaraṇaṁ aññāṇakaraṇaṁ paññānirodhikaṁ vighātapakkhiyaṁ anibbānasaṁvattanikaṁ.

text: Sensual desire,
pali: Kāmacchando, bhikkhave, āvaraṇo nīvaraṇo cetaso ajjhāruho paññāya dubbalīkaraṇo.

text: “In the same way, when the mind is restless, it’s the wrong time to develop the awakening factors of investigation of principles, energy, and rapture.
pali: “Evameva kho, bhikkhave, yasmiṁ samaye uddhataṁ cittaṁ hoti, akālo tasmiṁ samaye dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya, akālo vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya, akālo pītisambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya.

text: And what fuels the arising of ill will, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, āhāro anuppannassa vā byāpādassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā byāpādassa bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya?

text: They’re called a witless idiot because they haven’t developed and cultivated these seven awakening factors.”
pali: imesaṁ kho, bhikkhu, sattannaṁ bojjhaṅgānaṁ abhāvitattā abahulīkatattā ‘duppañño eḷamūgo’ti vuccatī”ti.

text: Why is that?
This body is sustained by food. It depends on food to continue, and without food it doesn’t continue.
pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, ayaṁ kāyo āhāraṭṭhitiko, āhāraṁ paṭicca tiṭṭhati, anāhāro no tiṭṭhati;

text: “Mendicants, when the perception of a skeleton is developed and cultivated it leads to dwelling in great ease.
pali: “Aṭṭhikasaññā, bhikkhave, bhāvitā bahulīkatā mahato phāsuvihārāya saṁvattati.

text: And what fuels the arising of sensual desire, or, when it has arisen, makes it increase and grow?
pali: Ko ca, bhikkhave, āhāro anuppannassa vā kāmacchandassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā kāmacchandassa bhiyyobhāvāya vepullāya?

text: fuels the arising of the awakening factor of rapture, or, when it has arisen, fully develops it.
pali: ayamāhāro anuppannassa vā pītisambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya, uppannassa vā pītisambojjhaṅgassa bhāvanāya pāripūriyā.

text: There is the feature of aversion.
If they wish: ‘May I meditate perceiving the repulsive in the unrepulsive,’ that’s what they do. …
pali: So sace ākaṅkhati ‘appaṭikūle paṭikūlasaññī vihareyyan’ti, paṭikūlasaññī tattha viharati …pe…

text: Indeed, Holy One, these are awakening factors!
There I see some ascetics and brahmins speaking for the sake of winning debates and finding fault.
pali: So tattha passāmi eke samaṇabrāhmaṇe itivādappamokkhānisaṁsañceva kathaṁ kathente upārambhānisaṁsañca:

text: and doubt.
pali: Vicikicchā, bhikkhave, āvaraṇā nīvaraṇā cetaso ajjhāruhā paññāya dubbalīkaraṇā.

text: Furthermore, when your heart is overcome and mired in doubt … Even hymns that are long-rehearsed don’t spring to mind, let alone those that are not rehearsed.
pali: Puna caparaṁ, brāhmaṇa, yasmiṁ samaye vicikicchāpariyuṭṭhitena cetasā viharati vicikicchāparetena, uppannāya ca vicikicchāya nissaraṇaṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti, attatthampi tasmiṁ samaye yathābhūtaṁ na jānāti na passati, paratthampi …pe… ubhayatthampi … dīgharattaṁ sajjhāyakatāpi mantā nappaṭibhanti, pageva asajjhāyakatā.

text: There are qualities that are skillful and unskillful, blameworthy and blameless, inferior and superior, and those with a portion of dark or bright.
pali: Atthi, bhikkhave, kusalākusalā dhammā sāvajjānavajjā dhammā hīnapaṇītā dhammā kaṇhasukkasappaṭibhāgā dhammā.

text: “Mendicant, they’re called a witless idiot because they haven’t developed and cultivated the seven awakening factors.
pali: “Sattannaṁ kho, bhikkhu, bojjhaṅgānaṁ abhāvitattā abahulīkatattā ‘duppañño eḷamūgo’ti vuccati.

text: It’s a mendicant who wields the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again … controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu anekavihitaṁ iddhividhaṁ paccanubhoti—ekopi hutvā bahudhā hoti …pe… yāva brahmalokāpi kāyena vasaṁ vatteti—

text: It’s when enthusiasm is coupled with laziness.
It’s when a mendicant develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm …
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu chandasamādhippadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ bhāveti,

text: He thinks: ‘My enthusiasm won’t be too lax or too tense. And it’ll be neither constricted internally nor scattered externally.’
pali: iti me chando na ca atilīno bhavissati, na ca atippaggahito bhavissati, na ca ajjhattaṁ saṅkhitto bhavissati, na ca bahiddhā vikkhitto bhavissati.

text: Six on slanting to the east,
“Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. …”
What do you think, mendicants?
but when you arrived at the park, the corresponding energy faded away?”
pali: Tassa te ārāmagatassa yaṁ tajjaṁ vīriyaṁ taṁ paṭippassaddhan”ti?

text: inquiry, and active effort.
And so there is this mental development, this immersion due to mental development, and these active efforts.
pali: Iti idañca cittaṁ, ayañca cittasamādhi, ime ca padhānasaṅkhārā—

text: mental development …
Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
pali: Atha kho bhagavā etamatthaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

text: Moggallāna develops the basis of psychic power that has immersion due to enthusiasm, and active effort.
pali: Idha, bhikkhave, moggallāno bhikkhu chandasamādhippadhānasaṅkhārasamannāgataṁ iddhipādaṁ bhāveti—

text: inquiry, and active effort.
All the ascetics and brahmins in the future who will partly manifest psychic powers will do so by developing and cultivating the four bases of psychic power.
pali: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, anāgatamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā iddhipadesaṁ abhinipphādessanti sabbe te catunnaṁ iddhipādānaṁ bhāvitattā bahulīkatattā.

text: as below, so above; as above, so below;
pali: yathā adho tathā uddhaṁ, yathā uddhaṁ tathā adho;

text: And so, with an open and unenveloped heart, he develops a mind that’s full of radiance.
pali: Iti vivaṭena cetasā apariyonaddhena sappabhāsaṁ cittaṁ bhāveti.

text: And so there is this enthusiasm, this immersion due to enthusiasm, and these active efforts.
pali: Iti ayañca chando, ayañca chandasamādhi, ime ca padhānasaṅkhārā—

text: It’s when enthusiasm is coupled with restlessness.
mind with delusion …
“Wanting to strike awe in you, the mendicant Moggallāna made the longhouse shake and rock and tremble with his big toe.
pali: “Tumheva kho, bhikkhave, saṁvejetukāmena moggallānena bhikkhunā pādaṅguṭṭhakena migāramātupāsādo, saṅkampito sampakampito sampacālito.

text: When the four bases of psychic power have been developed and cultivated in this way, they recollect many kinds of past lives. They remember: ‘There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.’ And so they recollect their many kinds of past lives, with features and details.

text: And what is the practice that leads to the development of the bases of psychic power?

right view, right purpose, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.

text: When the four bases of psychic power have been developed and cultivated in this way, they wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying themselves and becoming one again; materializing and dematerializing; going unobstructed through a wall, a rampart, or a mountain as if through space; diving in and out of the earth as if it were water; walking on water as if it were earth; flying cross-legged through the sky like a bird; touching and stroking with the hand the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful; controlling the body as far as the realm of divinity.
pali: Evaṁ bhāvitesu kho, bhikkhu, catūsu iddhipādesu evaṁ bahulīkatesu, anekavihitaṁ iddhividhaṁ paccanubhoti—ekopi hutvā bahudhā hoti, bahudhāpi hutvā eko hoti; āvibhāvaṁ, tirobhāvaṁ; tirokuṭṭaṁ tiropākāraṁ tiropabbataṁ asajjamāno gacchati, seyyathāpi ākāse; pathaviyāpi ummujjanimujjaṁ karoti, seyyathāpi udake; udakepi abhijjamāne gacchati, seyyathāpi pathaviyaṁ; ākāsepi pallaṅkena kamati, seyyathāpi pakkhī sakuṇo; imepi candimasūriye evaṁmahiddhike evaṁmahānubhāve pāṇinā parimasati parimajjati; yāva brahmalokāpi kāyena vasaṁ vatteti.
